metadata.title: Identification of Memorable Game Experiences: Analysis of Escape Rooms Visitors’ Experiences

abstract: Escape room is a type of game in which players have to solve several puzzles to leave the room in a limited time. It is a relatively new form of recreation but thanks to the global diffusion of innovations, the game has reached all continents over the decade, gaining unprecedented popularity, especially in large cities. The article aims to identify and characterize the attributes of memorable game experiences (MGEs) of escape room customers in Budapest, Hungary (the eldest in Europe, well-established market for this entertainment). The paper attempts to define the components of MGEs innovatively, using text-mining analysis of big data. This study used mixed methods: quantitative and qualitative. The research material consisted of 6203 opinions on experiences in 29 escape rooms in Budapest posted on TripAdvisor. The main components of positive experiences of users of escape rooms are puzzles as well as the arrangement and decoration. Other key components of the experience include staff, storytelling, the value of service, ambience, and atmosphere. Memorable game experiences are strongly saturated with emotions, resulting from the players' high involvement in the game and their full immersion in an artificially arranged problem situation. The obtained results indicate both the key areas necessary to create positive experiences and the elements resulting in the formation of negative experiences.

sections.paper_id: Nowacki and Stasiak - 2025 - Identification of Memorable Game Experiences Analysis of Escape Rooms Visitors’ Experiences
paragraphs: An escape room is a type of game in which players have to solve several puzzles to leave the room in a limited time (Nicholson, 2015;Stasiak, 2016). Escape rooms have gained immense popularity in recent years, captivating the minds of adventure enthusiasts and puzzle solvers alike. These immersive and interactive games present participants with thrilling challenges that test their problem-solving skills, teamwork, and ability to think under pressure. As escape rooms continue to evolve, it is crucial to understand what makes certain experiences within these games truly memorable.

Memorable game experiences (MGE) are those experiences created during the game that are memorable are selectively recalled from general experiences and can evoke strong emotions (both positive and negative). In the following article, the authors will focus on MGE experienced in escape rooms.

Previous studies of memorable experiences use survey methods, quantitative verification of structural models (Kim et al., 2012;Erkmen, 2019;Kim et al., 2019), web content analysis (Okumus et al., 2007), and literature reviews (Ellis et al., 2018;Rachao et al., 2020, Richards, 2021). Several gaps can be identified in the existing state of knowledge about MGEs. Existing scales fail to adequately capture what makes an experience memorable (Tung & Ritchie, 2011;Chandralal & Valenzuela, 2015;Lubbe et al., 2017;Coelho & Gosling, 2018). Therefore, unstructured techniques provide richer accounts of tourist experiences (Bosangit et al., 2015). There is still a lack of intercultural studies, a lack of identification of negative dimensions of memorable tourism experiences, a lack of use of mixed research methods (both quantitative and qualitative), and a lack of methods other than those using selfreporting measurements (Hosany et al., 2022). So far only Kolar (2017), Kolar and Cater (2018), and Dilek and Kulakoğlu Dilek (2018) conducted studies on the determinants and components of customer experiences in escape rooms and analyzed the content of TripAdvisor users' reviews.

The present article aims to identify and characterize the attributes of the MGEs of escape room customers based on a comprehensive analysis of the content of reviews posted on the TripAdvisor portal. The authors formulated the following research questions: What do players experience in escape rooms? What experiences do they remember from escape rooms? What attributes make the visit to the escape room memorable?

This article attempts to define and identify MGE for the first time. This was done innovatively by employing text-mining analysis of a very large amount of data (big data). Both positive and negative (which is new) memorable experiences in escape rooms were identified.

The contribution of the study consists in constructing a model of MGE attributes, which fits into a broader tendency to identify memorable leisure experiences (gastronomic, hospitality, destination, etc.). The obtained results contribute to the identification of the most important components and, in general, a better understanding of MGE in escape rooms. They should also be useful for the managers of this type of attraction in the process of improving the already provided services or designing an offer of completely new escape rooms.

sections.paper_id: Nowacki and Stasiak - 2025 - Identification of Memorable Game Experiences Analysis of Escape Rooms Visitors’ Experiences
Nowacki and Stasiak - 2025 - Identification of Memorable Game Experiences Analysis of Escape Rooms Visitors’ Experiences
paragraphs: According to LaSalle and Britton (2003, p. 38), experiences are "a product or service that, when combined with its surrounding experiences and events, goes beyond itself to enhance or bring value to a customer's life." In turn, Pine (2011) claims that memorable events engage each customer in an inherently personal way and thereby create a memory as the hallmark of that experience. Moreover, Pikkemaat et al. (2009) specify that these experiences are the result of encountering, undergoing, or living through situations that provide sensory, emotional, cognitive, behavioral, relational, and functional values. Thus, memorable experiences are events that are remembered and can evoke strong emotions (both positive and negative).

Over time, memorable experiences have been researched and written about, for example, in museums and heritage attractions (Kempiak et al., 2017;Nowacki & Kruczek, 2021), in restaurants and local food markets (Sthapit, 2017;Goolaup et al., 2019), in hotels and hospitality industry (Pijls et al., 2017;Sthapit, 2018;Sthapit & Jiménez-Barreto, 2018), in means of transport: on cruise ships (Radic, 2019;Hung & Wang, 2021) or in a sightseeing bus (Ghanem & Shaaban, 2022).

Memorable game experiences have not been explored in detail so far. Previous research has focused mainly on experiences-memorable moments-experienced during computer games such as Pokemon Go (Koskinen et al., 2019;Koskinen, Meriläinen, 2021). The authors analyzed the open-ended written responses and identified seven categories of MGE: gameplay & game content, people & sociability, location, circumstances & context, negative events, feelings, and other codes.

The experience in escape rooms is conceptualized by many researchers with the phenomena of immersion and flow. Flow is a mental state that arises in players act together on the same goal (solve difficult puzzles and leave the room) and are fully immersed in an artificially created world. It is "the state in which people are so involved in an activity that nothing else seems to matter" (Csikszentmihalyi, 1990, p. 4).

The flow theory shows that some activities, such as games and playing, offer better opportunities to experience flow than others. Gamification has recently been often used in tourism, also in the context of managing the tourist experience. The key elements of the games are a challenge, vivid emotions, involvement of participants, and their interactions with both the real and fictional worlds. All these factors together generate exciting entertainment that allows one to experience an unforgettable adventure (Harwood, & Garry, 2015;Stasiak, 2022). In addition, play means escapism: detaching from everyday duties and worries, suspension of real problems-even if only temporary, being outside time and space.

Van der Merwe and Saayman (2014) identified factors influencing a memorable game viewing experience during self-drive game viewing and making use of operators in safari vehicles for guided game viewing. Quantitative research using a web-based survey indicated that wildlife ambience and interpretation are fundamental in having memorable game-viewing experiences.

sections.paper_id: Nowacki and Stasiak - 2025 - Identification of Memorable Game Experiences Analysis of Escape Rooms Visitors’ Experiences
paragraphs: Despite the huge popularity of escape rooms all over the world, relatively few scientific papers devoted to this issue have been published. They appear in scientific circulation only after 2015. Villar Lama and García Martín (2021) found a total of 80 articles in the Scopus and Web of Science databases in which the term "escape room" was used.

Importantly, the vast majority of publications concern the principles of constructing escape rooms and using them to increase the attractiveness and effectiveness of classes at all levels of education (Nicholson, 2018;Reuter et al., 2020). Existing research shows the positive impact of this classroom technique on subject knowledge and cooperation skills (Sarage et al., 2021). The use of escape rooms significantly improves the achievement, motivation, commitment, and autonomy of students, it also significantly reduced anxiety related to learning. Those types of teaching tools are used especially not only in medical sciences: medicine (Kinio et al., 2017;Guo et al., 2020), nursing (Brown et al., 2019;Morrell & Ball 2020;Sarage et al., 2021), pharmacy (Eukel et al., 2017), science: mathematics (Fuentes-Cabrera et al., 2020), physics (Vörös & Sárközi, 2017), chemistry (Vergne et al., 2019), technical subjects (Giang et al., 2018;Tejado et al., 2021;Vidergor, 2021;Mystakidis & Christopoulos, 2022), biology (Christopoulous et al., 2023;Herm et al., 2022) but also in teaching foreign languages (López, 2019), literary studies (Armie et al., 2021), library studies (Davis, 2019;Heffernan & Chartier 2020), and even in sex education (von Kotzebue, 2022). However, teachers of different disciplines have different motives for using escape rooms, different ways of using game mechanics and pedagogical approaches, as well as different effects of using class gamification (Veldkamp et al., 2020a(Veldkamp et al., , 2020b;;Makri et al., 2021;Taraldsen et al., 2022, Buchner et al., 2022).

Educational escape rooms differ significantly from recreational escape rooms (in terms of location, arrangement, game organization, goals, and player experience). For scalability educational escape rooms are most commonly table-top games using software, physical padlocks, or electronic validation hardware. Therefore, the research conducted on them, and the results obtained cannot be easily transferred to escape rooms of a recreational nature. Despite the intensive development of the phenomenon, so far, only a dozen or so empirical works devoted to the analysis of recreational escape rooms have been published in the world. There are even fewer articles on their tourism function.

The first research work on escape rooms was an article by Nicholson (2015). The author, drawing upon a global survey with 175 owners of escape rooms from Europe, Asia, Australia, and both Americas, made a general description of the phenomenon: systematized the beginnings of the sector, described the strategies for the organization and operation of escape rooms and the specificity of their offer on different continents. Later works presented the results of empirical research on the operation of escape rooms in individual countries, including Poland (Stasiak, 2016), Turkey (Gündüz, 2018), Spain (Villar Lama, 2018), and Iran (Bakhsheshi, 2019).

Geographical studies (Stasiak, 2016;Villar Lama, 2018) focus on showing the spatial development of escape rooms from a dynamic (historical) perspective and on identifying their location factors. Both in Poland and in Spain, similar regularities in the diffusion of innovation were observed: escape rooms first appear in metropolises, then they expand to smaller cities, including tourist centers.

Another type of research concerns the role and importance of escape rooms in diversifying and enriching the city's cultural offer (Kubal & Pawlusin ́ski, 2016, based on the example of Krakow) or the creative sector (Nagy et al., 2018-in Budapest). Escape rooms are used not only by permanent residents of the metropolis but also by their temporary users: students, corporate employees, and tourists. These results show, on the one hand, the remarkable development of the ER sector, and on the other hand, a close relationship with urban tourism and recreation, and the millennial generation (dominant both among entrepreneurs and customers-Villar Lama & García Martín, 2021).

Currently, the biggest challenge is to create the so-called smart escape room, which can play a great role in nonformal education, for example, in museums or other places of cultural heritage. Such a room combines intelligent physical devices, digital technologies (AR, VR, and DS), and the Internet of Things with the concept of serious games (game-based learning) and the mechanics of escape rooms. This enables innovative and attractive shaping of the cultural experience in an engaging, educational, and entertaining way. Thanks to this, the game evokes a positive attitude to history, culture, and the visited place, and at the same time is an effective tool for modern interpretation of the area's heritage (Vaske, 2019;Tzima et al., 2021;Karageorgiou et al., 2022).

The third current of supply research focuses on the basics of creating an attractive escape room offer for customers, especially in the conditions of increasing competition in the market. The key factor of success is the skillful design of players' emotional experiences, including immersion (Julke-Bogacka, 2019) and flow, staff training, and innovative technological solutions (Pakhalov & Rozkova, 2020). Several publications indicated a poor variety of the offer in the escape room market-leading topics, stories, and tasks do not vary even between facilities in different countries (Gündüz, 2018;Pakhalov & Rozhkova 2020;Villar Lama & García Martin, 2021).

The demand research focuses primarily on the motivation, experiences, and emotions of escape room players. Most often they are netnographic and very rarely are carried out in real life, right after leaving the room-the end of the game (Pink et al., 2019). The findings of Stasiak (2019a) based on an online survey show that the motivation to visit escape rooms is related to the search for an unusual place to spend free time, the search for new things, and the desire to escape from everyday problems. The gameplay itself provides participants with vivid and often contradictory emotions that determine the quality and uniqueness of the experience.

The findings of Stasiak (2019a) based on an online survey show that the motivation to visit escape rooms is related to the search for an unusual place to spend free time, the search for new things, and the desire to escape from everyday problems. The gameplay itself provides participants with vivid and often contradictory emotions that determine the quality and uniqueness of the experience.
sections.text_with_footnotes:
Despite the huge popularity of escape rooms all over the world, relatively few scientific papers devoted to this issue have been published. They appear in scientific circulation only after 2015. Villar Lama and García Martín (2021) found a total of 80 articles in the Scopus and Web of Science databases in which the term "escape room" was used.

Importantly, the vast majority of publications concern the principles of constructing escape rooms and using them to increase the attractiveness and effectiveness of classes at all levels of education (Nicholson, 2018;Reuter et al., 2020). Existing research shows the positive impact of this classroom technique on subject knowledge and cooperation skills (Sarage et al., 2021). The use of escape rooms significantly improves the achievement, motivation, commitment, and autonomy of students, it also significantly reduced anxiety related to learning. Those types of teaching tools are used especially not only in medical sciences: medicine (Kinio et al., 2017;Guo et al., 2020), nursing (Brown et al., 2019;Morrell & Ball 2020;Sarage et al., 2021), pharmacy (Eukel et al., 2017), science: mathematics (Fuentes-Cabrera et al., 2020), physics (Vörös & Sárközi, 2017), chemistry (Vergne et al., 2019), technical subjects (Giang et al., 2018;Tejado et al., 2021;Vidergor, 2021;Mystakidis & Christopoulos, 2022), biology (Christopoulous et al., 2023;Herm et al., 2022) but also in teaching foreign languages (López, 2019), literary studies (Armie et al., 2021), library studies (Davis, 2019;Heffernan & Chartier 2020), and even in sex education (von Kotzebue, 2022). However, teachers of different disciplines have different motives for using escape rooms, different ways of using game mechanics and pedagogical approaches, as well as different effects of using class gamification (Veldkamp et al., 2020a(Veldkamp et al., , 2020b;;Makri et al., 2021;Taraldsen et al., 2022, Buchner et al., 2022).

Educational escape rooms differ significantly from recreational escape rooms (in terms of location, arrangement, game organization, goals, and player experience). For scalability educational escape rooms are most commonly table-top games using software, physical padlocks, or electronic validation hardware. Therefore, the research conducted on them, and the results obtained cannot be easily transferred to escape rooms of a recreational nature. Despite the intensive development of the phenomenon, so far, only a dozen or so empirical works devoted to the analysis of recreational escape rooms have been published in the world. There are even fewer articles on their tourism function.

The first research work on escape rooms was an article by Nicholson (2015). The author, drawing upon a global survey with 175 owners of escape rooms from Europe, Asia, Australia, and both Americas, made a general description of the phenomenon: systematized the beginnings of the sector, described the strategies for the organization and operation of escape rooms and the specificity of their offer on different continents. Later works presented the results of empirical research on the operation of escape rooms in individual countries, including Poland (Stasiak, 2016), Turkey (Gündüz, 2018), Spain (Villar Lama, 2018), and Iran (Bakhsheshi, 2019).

Geographical studies (Stasiak, 2016;Villar Lama, 2018) focus on showing the spatial development of escape rooms from a dynamic (historical) perspective and on identifying their location factors. Both in Poland and in Spain, similar regularities in the diffusion of innovation were observed: escape rooms first appear in metropolises, then they expand to smaller cities, including tourist centers.

Another type of research concerns the role and importance of escape rooms in diversifying and enriching the city's cultural offer (Kubal & Pawlusin ́ski, 2016, based on the example of Krakow) or the creative sector (Nagy et al., 2018-in Budapest). Escape rooms are used not only by permanent residents of the metropolis but also by their temporary users: students, corporate employees, and tourists. These results show, on the one hand, the remarkable development of the ER sector, and on the other hand, a close relationship with urban tourism and recreation, and the millennial generation (dominant both among entrepreneurs and customers-Villar Lama & García Martín, 2021).

Currently, the biggest challenge is to create the so-called smart escape room, which can play a great role in nonformal education, for example, in museums or other places of cultural heritage. Such a room combines intelligent physical devices, digital technologies (AR, VR, and DS), and the Internet of Things with the concept of serious games (game-based learning) and the mechanics of escape rooms. This enables innovative and attractive shaping of the cultural experience in an engaging, educational, and entertaining way. Thanks to this, the game evokes a positive attitude to history, culture, and the visited place, and at the same time is an effective tool for modern interpretation of the area's heritage (Vaske, 2019;Tzima et al., 2021;Karageorgiou et al., 2022).

The third current of supply research focuses on the basics of creating an attractive escape room offer for customers, especially in the conditions of increasing competition in the market. The key factor of success is the skillful design of players' emotional experiences, including immersion (Julke-Bogacka, 2019) and flow, staff training, and innovative technological solutions (Pakhalov & Rozkova, 2020). Several publications indicated a poor variety of the offer in the escape room market-leading topics, stories, and tasks do not vary even between facilities in different countries (Gündüz, 2018;Pakhalov & Rozhkova 2020;Villar Lama & García Martin, 2021).

The demand research focuses primarily on the motivation, experiences, and emotions of escape room players. Most often they are netnographic and very rarely are carried out in real life, right after leaving the room-the end of the game (Pink et al., 2019). The findings of Stasiak (2019a) based on an online survey show that the motivation to visit escape rooms is related to the search for an unusual place to spend free time, the search for new things, and the desire to escape from everyday problems. The gameplay itself provides participants with vivid and often contradictory emotions that determine the quality and uniqueness of the experience.

sections.paper_id: Nowacki and Stasiak - 2025 - Identification of Memorable Game Experiences Analysis of Escape Rooms Visitors’ Experiences
paragraphs: Budapest has become one of the world leaders in the ER market, and certainly the Central European capital of this entertainment. The first room (Para Park) was opened in 2011. However, the symbol of the Budapest escape rooms is "Claustrophilia"-a popular facility that the Hungarian term for this phenomenon is named after (Kubal & Pawlusin ́ski, 2016). From 2013, the number of escape rooms in the Hungarian capital began to grow rapidly. The first review on TripAdvisor was also published that year. In the second half of 2014, the portal offered 57 different locations (99 rooms) in Budapest, and in 2016-already 68 (154 rooms) (Nagy et al., 2018). In the Escape Room Director ranking, Budapest was ranked 9th among the cities with the largest number of escape rooms (but as the first non-Asian city) (French & Shaw, 2015).

Escape rooms in Budapest have been treated for years as an important element of the creative sector, determining the innovative nature of the city's offers. They are to be an alternative tourism proposal-a niche product for tourists with special interests, they are to present well-known tourist attractions in a way different from the usual, and finally, "draw" visitors away from the traditional areas of concentration of tourist traffic and direct them to other, less popular districts (Nagy et al., 2018).

Escape rooms are consistently promoted in Budapest in various ways: starting with giving players discount coupons for their next visit and encouraging them to share their opinions online (49 rooms were rated on TripAdvisor in 2016), to celebrating a night in escape rooms every October (from 2015), to the organization of the first world championship in this discipline in 2017, which competitors from over 40 countries came for. The offer of escape rooms for foreigners is constantly being expanded. The percentage of games that can be played in English increased from 31% to 42% in 2014-2016. As many as 52% of service providers of this type offered games in a foreign language. The service has become international (Nagy et al., 2018).

The birth of Budapest's escape rooms took place in the center of the metropolis, on the left bank of the Danube, in Pest, and more specifically in the former Jewish district of Erzsébetváros. Currently, it is a district of less known, often devastated monuments and an alternative youth entertainment culture (the so-called 'ruin pubs'), popular among foreigners. To this day, the largest concentration of Budapest escape rooms is in districts V-VIII (Figure 1). The phenomenon of situating escape rooms in the very center of the city is characteristic of Central European metropolises where there is a large supply of empty premises in old, low-standard tenement houses (Kubal & Pawlusin ́ski, 2016). The offer of escape rooms in Budapest is thematically varied. Nagy et al. (2018) distinguished seven main themes, but the vast majority of escape rooms (almost 47%) were simply about escaping from some unpleasant place (e.g., prison, basement, cabin, Egyptian tomb). Almost 28% of the rooms were devoted to conducting an investigation (catching a killer or thief, finding stolen jewellery, a missing person, or a valuable thing), and every tenth was dedicated to film topics (Miss Marple, Lost, Harry Potter, James Bond, Game of Thrones, C.S.I.: Crime Scene Investigation). There are also unusual game offers, such as a "mobile escape room" (converted bus or truck) or escape game in real city space. On the wave of successes, many Hungarian companies from the ER sector began to locate their activities in other countries of the region, for example, Budapest TRAP opened facilities based on a franchise agreement in the Berlin district of Friedrichshain (Kubal & Pawlusin ́ski, 2016).

sections.paper_id: Nowacki and Stasiak - 2025 - Identification of Memorable Game Experiences Analysis of Escape Rooms Visitors’ Experiences
paragraphs: This work is part of the demand trend of research on escape rooms. Its purpose is to identify the components of MGE based on a comprehensive analysis of reviews posted on the TripAdvisor portal. The research used quantitative (text mining) and qualitative (content analysis) methods. The research material consisted of opinions on experiences in escape rooms in Budapest. The web scraping technique using webscraper.io was used to retrieve all opinions (N = 6203) concerning all escape rooms in Budapest (n = 29) which contained at least 10 opinions (Figure 1).

The majority of opinions are 5* (Excellent)-5,488 (88.47%). There are much fewer opinions 4* (Very good)-499 (8.04%), 3* (Average)-120 (1.93%) and 2* (Poor) and 1* (Terrible)-both 48 opinions each (0.77%) (Table 1).

Before starting the actual analysis, all opinions were subjected to a data-cleaning procedure. By Knime Analytic Platform sequentially applied Punctuation Erasure, N Chars Filter (N > 2), Case Converter (all to lower case), Stop Word Filter, Number Filter, Stanford Tagger, and Stanford Lemmatizer.

The main topics appearing in the reviews were extracted with Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) using Knime. In LDA, each item of a collection is modeled as a finite mixture over an underlying set of topics, and each topic is modeled as an infinite mixture over an underlying set of topic probabilities (Blei et al., 2003). The disadvantage of the method is the necessity to assume a priori the number of topics. On the other hand, the advantage is that each document is assigned to a particular topic. The number of topics was determined using the elbow method (Gan & Qi, 2021) and calculating the perplexity and coherence factors using Python scripts. The elbow method consists in grouping the input data using the k-means method for a range of k values. Then, for each value of k, squared error sums (SSEs) are calculated, which are the sum of the distances of all data points to the respective cluster centers. Perplexity captures how surprised a model is by new data it has not seen before and is measured as the normalized log-likelihood of a held-out test set (Chen et al., 2018). The lower the perplexity value, the better the model fit. Topic Coherence measures the degree of semantic similarity between high-scoring words in the topic. The higher the value of topic coherence, the greater the internal consistency of the topic.

The sentiment analysis was carried out by applying the dictionary method with the SentiStrength 2.0 software. The significance of differences between the topics was verified using Pearson's Chi-square test. Content analysis was performed using NVivo 13.0 software.

sections.paper_id: Nowacki and Stasiak - 2025 - Identification of Memorable Game Experiences Analysis of Escape Rooms Visitors’ Experiences
Nowacki and Stasiak - 2025 - Identification of Memorable Game Experiences Analysis of Escape Rooms Visitors’ Experiences
sections.header_norm: quantitative analysis topic modeling with lda
paragraphs: The optimal number of topics was found by the elbow method. The optimal number of clusters for which SSEs decreased dramatically was 10. Then the values of the perplexity and coherency factors were calculated for 5-20 topics. The perplexity value decreases systematically from 5 to 20 topics, while the highest coherency value was for 10 topics. Hence, in further analysis, the 10-topics model was adopted as the optimal.

Latent Dirichlet Allocation analysis was performed for 10 topics and 20 words in each topic (alpha = 0.1 and beta = 0.001). Out of the 10 topics, one-relatively small (68 reviews-1.08%)-contained words in Hungarian, which excluded it from further analyses. The remaining topics were much more numerous and made it possible to formulate names based on the words they make up (Table 2).

Most reviews-1116 (18%)-were found in topic #8 "Great staff." Numerous words that describe delight dominate here, such as fun, great, recommend, good, nice, helpful, amazing, and super. Fewer opinions-1048 (16.9%)-were found in topic #4 "Great game experience." Words such as nice, staff, helpful, good, and fun appear again but also exciting, enjoy, and interesting, which apply to the game itself. And the third most numerous topic is #8 "Fun time" with 862 (13.9%) opinions. The least numerous topic was #2 "Scary experience-one actor," containing words such as scary, actor, horror, and guy, regarding inappropriate behavior of persons supervising the course of the game.
The analysis of the ranking of opinions collected in individual topics showed significant differences between them (Pearson's Chi 2 = 1302.05, df = 36, p < 0.001).

Three topics with very positive feedback were identified (Table 3): #1, #4, and #7. In topic #1 ("Best escape room in Budapest"), which contains 12.99% of all reviews, contains 13.9% of all 5* reviews, 0% of 1* reviews, and only 2% of 2* reviews. Similarly, topic #4 ("Great game experience"), which contains 18.1% of all opinions, contains 18.5% of 5* and 0% and 2% of 1* and 2* opinions. Topic #7 ("Great staff") composed of 18% of opinions, it contains as much as 19.4% of 5* opinions and 0% and 2% of 2* and 1* opinions. Two topics with very negative feedback were also identified: #2 and #6. Topic #2 ("Scary experience-one actor") with only 3.4% of all opinions contains as much as 18.7% of all 1* opinions and 10.4% of 2* opinions. Topic #6 ("Room with puzzle game") contains the most negative opinions: with only 6.9% of all opinions, it contains the majority of 1, 2, and 3* opinions-60.4%, 70.8%, and 56.6%. To verify whether the above-received topics have the most positive and the most negative emotional dimension, sentiment analysis was performed. As a result, it was found that topics #1 ("Best escape room in Budapest"), #4 ("Great game experience"), and #7 ("Great staff") had the most positive sentiment, and topics #2 ("Scary experience-one actor") and #6 ("Room with puzzle game") had the most negative (Table 4), which confirmed the analysis of the opinion ranking (Table 3). The analysis of the standard deviation of sentiment shows that players are very consistent in their opinions about positive emotions, which are manifested in low standard deviation (0.73, 0.62, and 0.63). On the other hand, negative topics have very high standard deviations (1.43 and 0.99), which means the high emotional heterogeneity of these opinions.

The analysis of the ranking of opinions collected in individual topics showed significant differences between them (Pearson's Chi 2 = 1302.05, df = 36, p < 0.001).

Three topics with very positive feedback were identified (Table 3): #1, #4, and #7. In topic #1 ("Best escape room in Budapest"), which contains 12.99% of all reviews, contains 13.9% of all 5* reviews, 0% of 1* reviews, and only 2% of 2* reviews. Similarly, topic #4 ("Great game experience"), which contains 18.1% of all opinions, contains 18.5% of 5* and 0% and 2% of 1* and 2* opinions. Topic #7 ("Great staff") composed of 18% of opinions, it contains as much as 19.4% of 5* opinions and 0% and 2% of 2* and 1* opinions. Two topics with very negative feedback were also identified: #2 and #6. Topic #2 ("Scary experience-one actor") with only 3.4% of all opinions contains as much as 18.7% of all 1* opinions and 10.4% of 2* opinions. Topic #6 ("Room with puzzle game") contains the most negative opinions: with only 6.9% of all opinions, it contains the majority of 1, 2, and 3* opinions-60.4%, 70.8%, and 56.6%. To verify whether the above-received topics have the most positive and the most negative emotional dimension, sentiment analysis was performed. As a result, it was found that topics #1 ("Best escape room in Budapest"), #4 ("Great game experience"), and #7 ("Great staff") had the most positive sentiment, and topics #2 ("Scary experience-one actor") and #6 ("Room with puzzle game") had the most negative (Table 4), which confirmed the analysis of the opinion ranking (Table 3). The analysis of the standard deviation of sentiment shows that players are very consistent in their opinions about positive emotions, which are manifested in low standard deviation (0.73, 0.62, and 0.63). On the other hand, negative topics have very high standard deviations (1.43 and 0.99), which means the high emotional heterogeneity of these opinions.

sections.paper_id: Nowacki and Stasiak - 2025 - Identification of Memorable Game Experiences Analysis of Escape Rooms Visitors’ Experiences
paragraphs: In content analysis, the opinions from topics containing the most MGEs (i.e., with the strongest positive or negative sentiment) were analyzed. Topics with the most positive (#1-"Best escape room in Budapest," #7-"Great staff," and #4-"Great game experience") and the most negative sentiment (#2-"Scary experience-one actor" and #6-"Room with puzzle game") were analyzed. Identified factors of MGE are presented graphically in Figure 2.

Topic #1 ("Best escape room in Budapest") is very positive (13.96% positive, 3.25 positive sentiment-806 opinions [12.99%]). The opinions that are included in it usually contain words such as room, escape, best, budapest, game, do, recommend, hell, great, heaven, play, puzzle, fun, amazing, experience, try, definitely, visit, far, and love.

The first two identified factors of MGE were puzzles and decoration: "Best one so far (…) if there are still rooms that can surprise you-both with puzzles and decoration." And in other opinions: "The puzzles were so ingenious and the sets were absolutely amazing" and "There were so many details and decorations which made the experience even better."

Another identified factor was the storyline: "The story was awesome and very well written." or "Heaven & Hell story is one of the best I've seen." Another opinion even points to the key role of the story in shaping MGE: "The escape games are story driven which is a nice twist to just crazy puzzle filled rooms." Not only the story itself is important but especially entire the storyline: "(…) has a very unique storyline that I personally really enjoyed." Topic #2 ("Scary experience-one actor") very negative (18.75%-1*, 10.42%-2*), most negative sentiment (-2.96, mean 0.07), small size-3.45% (214 opinions). The most common words are experience, scary, feel, actor, amazing, go, good, horror, house, real, budapest, guy, think, show, tell, thing, know, want, best, and friend. In this topic, there are mainly negative factors, which are Staf and game master: "We did not find it very scary and was verging on boring. Near the end of the experience one of the actors slammed my head down on a table and asked, "is it scary now?" The author complains a lot about the staff-especially about one "actor," who behaved scandalously and destroyed the entire "escape room experience" with his behavior. Similarly, in another opinion, also one "actor" in service caused very negative emotions through scandalous behavior: "The whole experience proved to be a massive let-down, as a result of one of the actors inside-his behavior was completely unacceptable, and verged on physical abuse." Reviewers complained not only about the behavior but also about the language of the staff: "I am amused by the show but one of the guys acting in the show has offensive disturbing language irrelevant to the show. National stereotypes-racist jokes."

Topic #4 ("Great game experience")-extremely positive (18.15%-5*, 3.32-positive sentiment, 1.90-sum) and very large segment (16.91%). The most characteristic words for this topic are game, room, great, experience, time, escape, nice, staff, exciting, theme, helpful, recommend, good, kind, amazing, fun, enjoy, best, thank, and interesting. The following factors are crucial for a great gaming experience: riddles, atmosphere, setup, and decoration. For example: "Nice riddles (…) nice setup, awesome atmosphere" and "We literally escaped as the timer ticked over so the atmosphere created was fantastic." In another opinion, they mention ambience and decorations: "SteamPunk ambience and decorations are really nice."

An important factor of MGE is also the theme: "The room theme is amazing." or "We really enjoyed the Game of Thrones theme and all the game elements that came along with that." It should also be noted that what about other attractions can be considered pejorative opinions in the case of escape rooms has a positive connotation: "Horrifying! The actors certainly commit to the theme, I was relieved and pleased when it finished, but we both really enjoyed it."

Topic #6 ("Room with puzzle game") negative (60.42%-1*, 70.83%-2*, 56.57-3*, very negative sentiment--2.29) and small segment-6.98%. The most characteristic words in this topic are room, game, puzzle, time, nice, ask, give, hint, need, escape, solve, good, help, work, lock, play, thing, clue, bit, and minute.

Value for money was often mentioned in this topic: "The escape room didn't have a high production value. It was fine for the price." or "Game was missing a key AND one lock was just hanging open on a door, making this the worst game we have played in Budapest. Of course, they didn't forget to charge full price. Making mistakes is one thing, not providing some sort of compensation can not be forgiven." There were also frequent complaints about the game master and customer service: "We (…) were really disappointed how the game master treated us and the situation. She had to come in many times (at least 4-5 times) during the game, because of several problems and unfunctionality in the room and didn't even apologize about it!! In addition to that, we couldn't even communicate with her because the woki toki didn't work at all." or "The main reason why we won't return to this company is the attitude of the staff after the escape. We were neglected as there was another group waiting for a game." Topic #7 ("Great staff")-most positive (19.44%-5*), very positive sentiment (3.26), biggest topic-18%. The topic was characterized by words: room, fun, great, staff, escape, experience, recommend, time, friendly, good, nice, helpful, killer, definitely, amazing, friend, try, super, serial, and budapest.

According to the title of the topic, most opinions concerned staff: "The staff was very friendly and the rooms were done amazingly." or "David was a brilliant host who really ramped up the atmosphere before and after." This topic contains reviews that complement escape rooms' staff, and generally, people who work there: "Great room, amazing service. The place is great, and the attention was amazing. Adrián and Marietta were super nice and super fun." or "Our first impression stayed with the friendliness and supportiveness of the staff. A brief and clear explanation of the games, together with the burning enthusiasm of them made us cannot help but to feel pumped up even before attending the actual game."

sections.paper_id: Nowacki and Stasiak - 2025 - Identification of Memorable Game Experiences Analysis of Escape Rooms Visitors’ Experiences
paragraphs: Escape rooms are a relatively new and little-explored attraction in the leisure market. They are an alluring offer, especially for city users: permanent residents, temporary residents, and tourists. Previous studies (Kolar, 2017;Kolar & C ̌ater, 2018;Dilek & Kulakoğlu Dilek, 2018) prove that we are dealing with completely new, peak, unique, and fun experiences, and their source is on the one hand solving difficult puzzles, and on the other hand-working in a team (social component). For customers of escape rooms, the following are particularly important: fun, emotions, challenges, achievements, and the unique atmosphere of adventure. Escapers have fun when visiting escape rooms and are in the flow "with someone" (collective flow experience) rather than individually (Dilek & Kulakoğlu Dilek, 2018). Kolar and C ̌ater (2018) emphasize that we are dealing with a unique group flow experience here.

Contemporary tourists are seekers of authentic and hedonistic experiences, who constantly demand new products, attractions, and events that will be able to meet their growing demands. Escape rooms are a perfect response to these expectations. Dilek and Kulakoğlu Dilek (2018) found them to be an important phenomenon for the conceptual development, research, and management of playful experiences in urban, creative, and special interest tourism. Therefore, each research expanding our knowledge about those venues and their users is valuable.

The present study makes several original contributions through its research methodology and findings. First, the paper introduces an innovative approach to defining the components of MGEs in escape rooms. It employs text-mining analysis of big data to identify and characterize the attributes of MGEs. This method allows for the quantitative exploration of a large dataset combined with qualitative insights from user opinions.

Second, the study utilizes a mixed methods approach, combining quantitative and qualitative research techniques. By analyzing all 6203 opinions on experiences in all 29 escape rooms in Budapest, the authors obtain a comprehensive understanding of the elements that contribute to MGEs. The study used complete data on all escape rooms located in a large city, which allowed us to obtain a full picture of MGE obtained in this city (e.g., Kolar (2017) conducted similar research only for two escape rooms).

Third, the paper identifies and characterizes the main components of positive experiences reported by escape room users. These components include the storyline, staff and game master, puzzles and riddles, ambience, atmosphere and decoration, theme, and service and value for money (fig. 2). This categorization provides a framework for understanding the key areas necessary for creating positive game experiences. Pakhalov and Rozhkova (2020) found that the escape room's atmosphere (ambience) is the most frequently mentioned positive cue that shapes the visitors' experience. In turn, in the research of Kolar (2017) most frequently mentioned concepts were "fun," Dilek and Kulakoğlu Dilek (2018)-"game," and Stasiak (2019b)-"puzzle." Among other components of a positive experience, Pakhalov and Rozhkova (2020) mentioned: puzzles, game master, story, teamwork, customer orientation, brainpower, fun, and thus largely coinciding with the presented research. On the other hand, Kolar and C ̌ater (2018) presented surprising results. According to them, the staff does not significantly affect the experience of visitors to escape rooms. They stated that game masters are invisible intermediaries of experience, who are primarily to ensure safety (in case of panic) and provide participants with minimal guidance and hints. In the third generation escape rooms, however, the role of the game master is much larger: often in an appropriate disguise and make-up, he explains the plot to the players, vividly tells and comments on the happening story, and also plays specific scenes to invigorate the game.

At the same time, Kolar and C ̌ater (2018) found that theming and storytelling engage participants in the game, add drama, and promote the creation of hyperreality. Their study supports the view that both challenging tasks and a symbolic-themed environment contribute to customers' immersive experiences. Creating an alternative world, fully involving the players, is created, among others, by multilevel scenery design, authentic props, modern technical solutions, as well as special effects (visual and sound) (Stasiak, 2022). The use of sophisticated technology can change the atmosphere of a room, resulting in a more authentic experience and a deeper immersion of visitors in the game. Pakhalov and Rozhkova (2020) even emphasize the need to incur significant expenses on special effects and virtual reality.

Fourthly, in addition to positive experiences, the paper also investigates the elements that contribute to the formation of negative experiences. By identifying these factors, such as poor storyline, unhelpful staff, unsatisfying puzzles, and subpar ambience, the study provides insights into areas that escape room operators should address to prevent negative experiences. The main cause of negative experiences of escape room users in Budapest is the staff (horrible customer service)-inappropriate, amateurish, or tactless behavior of service staff, especially the game master. This confirms the key role (for the customers' satisfaction) of suitably qualified employees in this sector, who have the so-called experiential intelligence (Sotiriadis & Varvaressos, 2016). A particularly sought-after competence of tourism employees is the ability to stage brand experiences (based, among others, on strong empathy, knowledge of the psychology of emotions and memory, creativity, and improvisation skills) (Stasiak, 2019b). Kolar and C ̌ater (2018) emphasize that in the case of escape rooms, game masters' interventions may sometimes be correct, but not always justified, as they adversely affect the spontaneity and fluidity of the game, and, consequently, the final satisfaction of the customers. TripAdvisor reviewers also cited other annoying, completely negative experiences. These were technical faults (failures) as well as unsatisfactory value for money. In the article by Pakhalov and Rozhkova (2020), similar elements were mentioned among the components of a negative experience in escape rooms, for example, game master, puzzles, technologies, atmosphere, and booking.

It should be noted that the game master (more broadly staff) and technologies (new technological solutions) have been identified as components of both positive and negative experiences. If they function perfectly, they contribute to the satisfaction of players, but if they fail, they cause even greater disappointment, dissatisfaction, and even anger in the users of escape rooms.

Fifth, the research findings highlight that MGEs in escape rooms are strongly saturated with emotions. The high involvement of players in the game and their full immersion in the artificially arranged problem situation contribute to the formation of these emotions. This insight emphasizes the importance of designing experiences that elicit strong emotional responses from participants.

Overall, this paper contributes to the field of escape room design and customer experience by offering an innovative approach to identifying and characterizing the components of MGEs. sections.text:
Escape rooms are a relatively new and little-explored attraction in the leisure market. They are an alluring offer, especially for city users: permanent residents, temporary residents, and tourists. Previous studies (Kolar, 2017;Kolar & C ̌ater, 2018;Dilek & Kulakoğlu Dilek, 2018) prove that we are dealing with completely new, peak, unique, and fun experiences, and their source is on the one hand solving difficult puzzles, and on the other hand-working in a team (social component). For customers of escape rooms, the following are particularly important: fun, emotions, challenges, achievements, and the unique atmosphere of adventure. Escapers have fun when visiting escape rooms and are in the flow "with someone" (collective flow experience) rather than individually (Dilek & Kulakoğlu Dilek, 2018). Kolar and C ̌ater (2018) emphasize that we are dealing with a unique group flow experience here.

Contemporary tourists are seekers of authentic and hedonistic experiences, who constantly demand new products, attractions, and events that will be able to meet their growing demands. Escape rooms are a perfect response to these expectations. Dilek and Kulakoğlu Dilek (2018) found them to be an important phenomenon for the conceptual development, research, and management of playful experiences in urban, creative, and special interest tourism. Therefore, each research expanding our knowledge about those venues and their users is valuable.

The present study makes several original contributions through its research methodology and findings. First, the paper introduces an innovative approach to defining the components of MGEs in escape rooms. It employs text-mining analysis of big data to identify and characterize the attributes of MGEs. This method allows for the quantitative exploration of a large dataset combined with qualitative insights from user opinions.

Second, the study utilizes a mixed methods approach, combining quantitative and qualitative research techniques. By analyzing all 6203 opinions on experiences in all 29 escape rooms in Budapest, the authors obtain a comprehensive understanding of the elements that contribute to MGEs. The study used complete data on all escape rooms located in a large city, which allowed us to obtain a full picture of MGE obtained in this city (e.g., Kolar (2017) conducted similar research only for two escape rooms).

Third, the paper identifies and characterizes the main components of positive experiences reported by escape room users. These components include the storyline, staff and game master, puzzles and riddles, ambience, atmosphere and decoration, theme, and service and value for money (fig. 2). This categorization provides a framework for understanding the key areas necessary for creating positive game experiences. Pakhalov and Rozhkova (2020) found that the escape room's atmosphere (ambience) is the most frequently mentioned positive cue that shapes the visitors' experience. In turn, in the research of Kolar (2017) most frequently mentioned concepts were "fun," Dilek and Kulakoğlu Dilek (2018)-"game," and Stasiak (2019b)-"puzzle." Among other components of a positive experience, Pakhalov and Rozhkova (2020) mentioned: puzzles, game master, story, teamwork, customer orientation, brainpower, fun, and thus largely coinciding with the presented research. On the other hand, Kolar and C ̌ater (2018) presented surprising results. According to them, the staff does not significantly affect the experience of visitors to escape rooms. They stated that game masters are invisible intermediaries of experience, who are primarily to ensure safety (in case of panic) and provide participants with minimal guidance and hints. In the third generation escape rooms, however, the role of the game master is much larger: often in an appropriate disguise and make-up, he explains the plot to the players, vividly tells and comments on the happening story, and also plays specific scenes to invigorate the game.

At the same time, Kolar and C ̌ater (2018) found that theming and storytelling engage participants in the game, add drama, and promote the creation of hyperreality. Their study supports the view that both challenging tasks and a symbolic-themed environment contribute to customers' immersive experiences. Creating an alternative world, fully involving the players, is created, among others, by multilevel scenery design, authentic props, modern technical solutions, as well as special effects (visual and sound) (Stasiak, 2022). The use of sophisticated technology can change the atmosphere of a room, resulting in a more authentic experience and a deeper immersion of visitors in the game. Pakhalov and Rozhkova (2020) even emphasize the need to incur significant expenses on special effects and virtual reality.

Fourthly, in addition to positive experiences, the paper also investigates the elements that contribute to the formation of negative experiences. By identifying these factors, such as poor storyline, unhelpful staff, unsatisfying puzzles, and subpar ambience, the study provides insights into areas that escape room operators should address to prevent negative experiences. The main cause of negative experiences of escape room users in Budapest is the staff (horrible customer service)-inappropriate, amateurish, or tactless behavior of service staff, especially the game master. This confirms the key role (for the customers' satisfaction) of suitably qualified employees in this sector, who have the so-called experiential intelligence (Sotiriadis & Varvaressos, 2016). A particularly sought-after competence of tourism employees is the ability to stage brand experiences (based, among others, on strong empathy, knowledge of the psychology of emotions and memory, creativity, and improvisation skills) (Stasiak, 2019b). Kolar and C ̌ater (2018) emphasize that in the case of escape rooms, game masters' interventions may sometimes be correct, but not always justified, as they adversely affect the spontaneity and fluidity of the game, and, consequently, the final satisfaction of the customers. TripAdvisor reviewers also cited other annoying, completely negative experiences. These were technical faults (failures) as well as unsatisfactory value for money. In the article by Pakhalov and Rozhkova (2020), similar elements were mentioned among the components of a negative experience in escape rooms, for example, game master, puzzles, technologies, atmosphere, and booking.

It should be noted that the game master (more broadly staff) and technologies (new technological solutions) have been identified as components of both positive and negative experiences. If they function perfectly, they contribute to the satisfaction of players, but if they fail, they cause even greater disappointment, dissatisfaction, and even anger in the users of escape rooms.

Fifth, the research findings highlight that MGEs in escape rooms are strongly saturated with emotions. The high involvement of players in the game and their full immersion in the artificially arranged problem situation contribute to the formation of these emotions. This insight emphasizes the importance of designing experiences that elicit strong emotional responses from participants.

Overall, this paper contributes to the field of escape room design and customer experience by offering an innovative approach to identifying and characterizing the components of MGEs. The findings provide valuable insights for escape room operators and game designers to create more engaging, immersive, and enjoyable experiences for their customers.

sections.paper_id: Nowacki and Stasiak - 2025 - Identification of Memorable Game Experiences Analysis of Escape Rooms Visitors’ Experiences
sections.header_raw: Limitations, Future Research, and Practical Implications
sections.header_norm: limitations future research and practical implications
paragraphs: This study is not free from several important limitations which largely result from its exploratory nature and the netnographic method applied. The research sample-although relatively large-cannot be treated as representative of the entire population of visitors to escape rooms in Budapest. The analyzed reviews come only from the Internet source-so they do not represent the opinion of people who are inactive on the Internet. Therefore, in the study, we are probably dealing with an overrepresentation of young and middle-aged reviewers (e-WOM generation).

It takes time, commitment, and determination to publish one's review on TripAdvisor. It can be assumed that making this effort is caused by vivid emotions (positive or negative) and the desire to share one's opinion with others. This naturally limits the moderate "lukewarm" ratings. According to Yang (2017), there are three basic predictors of intention to use electronic word of mouth: the experience factor (satisfaction), knowledge sharing factors (selfish and altruistic needs), and technology acceptance factors (perceived usefulness and perceived ease of use).

The specified limitations also indicate the desired directions of future research. Certainly, it would be cognitively interesting to examine the omitted (unrepresented) groups of escape room customers, that is, non-English-speaking players, inactive on the Internet, moderately satisfied, and then compare their experiences with the experiences of TripAdvisor reviewers. There may be statistically significant differences here. It would be intriguing to compare the experiences of users of escape rooms in various cities around the world in the context of cultural differences. It would also be valuable to discover the correlation between the personal characteristics of players (motivations, demographic variables, origins) and the nature of experiences derived from escape rooms. Previous studies have treated escape rooms en bloc, not paying much attention to the variety of topics, the nature of the game, the types of puzzles, etc. And undoubtedly, it can also have a big impact on the shape of MGE. Finally, greater use of quantitative methods (or a combination of quantitative and qualitative methods) is also desirable to more accurately and reliably identify the structural elements of the experience and the relationship between its dimensions.

This research provides important implications for better understanding and managing the customer experience in the escape room market. The results obtained in the content analysis were used to identify the key components of the memorable experience. For the owners and designers of escape rooms (not only in Budapest), practical tips are also important regarding the areas (problems) that should be paid special attention to when planning and creating positive experiences. At the same time, the respondents indicated the most irritating, totally negative experiences. Such weaknesses of escape rooms should be eliminated effectively (and permanently) by the managers as quickly as possible.

However, the problem of integrating the MGE into the overall memorable destination experience remains a challenge for the future to-on the one hand-provide tourists with unique entertainment and satisfying fun, and on the other hand-to attempt to interpret the local cultural heritage attractively. Escape rooms can be used by Destination Managers (Destination Management Organisations) to create a comprehensive strategy for the development of game tourism in the city. Gamification can be a driving force of destination branding, especially in emerging markets.
